title: Is the zero-divisor graph Γ(Z_13 × Z_25) prime?
generalization_signal: The four-class Γ(Z_p × Z_25) line now survives the first p = 13 frontier, but only after refining the old upper-half-prime C-block subtemplate: two extra primes 37 and 41 must be admitted below half the interval, with their doubles 74 and 82 pushed into D rather than kept out of the whole labeling.
strongest_honest_claim: An explicit prime-labeling witness for Γ(Z_13 × Z_25) survives skeptical verification. The campaign-relevant gain is not just another exact feeder: it shows the F25 line does not break at p = 13, but the unchanged upper-half-prime-only C program is too rigid and must be replaced by a refined prime-block lemma that allows a bounded number of doubled-prime exceptions to land in D.
candidate_theorem_slice: A refined support-template theorem for odd primes p on the four-class Γ(Z_p × Z_25) line: if the interval {1, ..., 5p + 19} contains p - 1 primes for C whose nontrivial multiples stay out of A ∪ B, and if B is a four-label pairwise-coprime barrier set whose nontrivial multiples fit inside the 20 A-slots, then Γ(Z_p × Z_25) is prime.
next_action: Update the zero-divisor family dossier around the refined F25(p) C-block lemma, then run Γ(Z_13 × Z_13 × Z_2) as the next decisive feeder before spending Lean effort on this exact instance.
